denial_pattern_sources: missing_iro_notice: CMS External Appeal Regulations 45 CFR § 147.136; ACA § 2719
denial_letter_text:
Dear Plan Member,

We received your provider's request on 06/24/2026 for Eculizumab (Soliris) related to Myasthenia gravis, generalized (G70.00). After review, we cannot approve this request.

Our review applied UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines and Optum clinical criteria. The documentation submitted does not meet medical necessity criteria because the service is not medically necessary for this member at this time. Eculizumab (Soliris) is non-preferred; a formulary alternative must be tried for Myasthenia gravis, generalized (G70.00). A lower-tier agent is available without demonstrated failure of the preferred option.

Specifically, the information provided does not satisfy the utilization-management requirements associated with formulary tier dispute. The clinical notes reviewed did not include sufficient detail to confirm that plan criteria were met. The requesting provider may submit additional records, but the current file does not support approval.

If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, they may contact our physician review line. Any discussion must occur within the timeframe described in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination through our internal appeals process. To file a standard appeal, send a written request and supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification number and the service reference shown on your explanation of benefits.

Sincerely,

UnitedHealthcare Utilization Management
clinical_context: The 57-year-old member has Myasthenia gravis, generalized (G70.00). She has been treated under commercial coverage with Eculizumab (Soliris) recommended as the next appropriate step. Positive acetylcholine receptor antibodies; failed pyridostigmine and steroids. This directly contradicts the insurer's position that formulary tier dispute bars coverage: the chart documents dates, doses, and objective findings (including dated scale scores in the chart) that were submitted with the original request but are not reflected in the denial letter's rationale. Treating clinicians note ongoing functional impairment and avoidable harm if her care is delayed further.
